column3: IN THE FIRST YEAR OF CYRUS KING OF THE PERSIANS] that is, in the year in which Cyrus transferred the monarchy from the Chaldeans to the Persians. This first year of Cyrus was therefore not the first year of the Persian kingdom, i.e., the year in which he was first made king and began to reign in Persia (from which he reigned a full thirty years, as Eusebius and Africanus hold in their Chronicles, St. Jerome on Zach. ch. 10, Pererius on Daniel ch. 5, and others); but this first year of Cyrus refers to the monarchy and kingdom of Babylon, namely the year in which, having defeated Belshazzar and captured and overthrown Babylon, he transferred the monarchy from the Chaldeans to himself and the Persians. For he could not have rescued the Jews from Babylon and from the hands of the Chaldeans unless he had first overcome and conquered them. This is clearly shown by the words of Cyrus in v. 2.

Many honourable and laudable works did Nehemiah perform; and having grown old he died, a man born to virtue and justice, and most beneficent toward his own people, leaving behind an everlasting monument of himself in the walls of Jerusalem.
Zerubbabel, however, remained in Babylon, and died there, as the Hebrews record in the Seder Olam.

QUESTION: Who and what manner of man was Nehemiah? Severus Sulpicius, St. Isidore, and Sixtus of Siena in their writings on Nehemiah consider him to have been of the tribe of Judah. Yet that he was a priest, and therefore descended from the tribe of Levi, is clear from 2 Maccabees 2:21. Furthermore, he himself was a courtier of Artaxerxes, indeed his cupbearer; whence he obtained from him permission to rebuild the city, and this he accomplished with great labor and great fortitude, continually contending with enemies who sought to impede the construction. This Artaxerxes was Longimanus, son of Xerxes, under whom the High Priest of the Jews was Eliashib son of Joakim, grandson of Jeshua, a close associate of Nehemiah and his helper. Moreover, Nehemiah, beyond this book, wrote another concerning the events that befell the Jews after their return to Jerusalem, mention of which is made in 2 Maccabees 2:13: "And the same things were reported in the writings and commentaries of Nehemiah; and how he, founding a library, gathered together out of the countries the books both of the prophets, and of David, and the epistles of the kings, and concerning the holy gifts."
SECOND QUESTION: Whether this Nehemiah is the same as

Some think they are different persons: they prove this first, because that Ezra was one of the leaders of those returning from captivity under Cyrus; wherefore at that time he was at least twenty years of age. But from the first year of Cyrus to the thirty-second year of Artaxerxes Longimanus, which our Nehemiah here reached,

Nehemiah was at least one hundred and sixteen years old, and therefore unfit for the office of cupbearer to Artaxerxes, and much more unfit for that laborious embassy and journey from Jerusalem to Persia to Artaxerxes, concerning which

On that day they read in the book of Moses in the audience of the people; and therein was found written, that the Ammonite and the Moabite should not come into the congregation of God for ever. Note that Esdras here speaks of himself in the first person, but concerning Nehemiah the companion of Esdras, Scripture speaks in the third person.
Because they met not the children of Israel with bread and with water, but hired Balaam against them, that he should curse them: howbeit our God turned the curse into a blessing.
Now it came to pass, when they had heard the law, that they separated from Israel all the mixed multitude.

And before this, Eliashib the priest, having the oversight of the chamber of the house of our God, was allied unto Tobiah. Note that Eliashib the high priest had given to Tobiah the Ammonite, the enemy of Israel, a great chamber in the temple, which was contrary to the law just read.

And he had prepared for him a great chamber, where aforetime they laid the meat offerings, the frankincense, and the vessels, and the tithes of the corn, the new wine, and the oil, which was commanded to be given to the Levites, and the singers, and the porters; and the offerings of the priests. This chamber had previously been used as a storehouse for the tithes and sacred vessels of the temple.

But in all this time was not I at Jerusalem: for in the two and thirtieth year of Artaxerxes king of Babylon came I unto the king, and after certain days obtained I leave of the king. Nehemiah had been absent from Jerusalem, having returned to Artaxerxes; now he returns and finds these abuses.
And I came to Jerusalem, and understood of the evil that Eliashib did for Tobiah, in preparing him a chamber in the courts of the house of God.

And it grieved me sore: therefore I cast forth all the household stuff of Tobiah out of the chamber. Nehemiah, burning with zeal for God's house, cast out all of Tobiah's goods, as Christ later cast out the money-changers from the temple. # GEM
Then I commanded, and they cleansed the chambers: and thither brought I again the vessels of the house of God, with the meat offering and the frankincense.

And I perceived that the portions of the Levites had not been given them: for the Levites and the singers, that did the work, were fled every one to his field. Because the tithes were not being paid, the Levites had abandoned the temple service and returned to farming to sustain themselves.

Then contended I with the rulers, and said, Why is the house of God forsaken? And I gathered them together, and set them in their place. Nehemiah rebukes the magistrates who had neglected to provide for the Levites, and restores the sacred ministers to their posts.
Then brought all Judah the tithe of the corn and the new wine and the oil unto the treasuries. The people, moved by Nehemiah's zeal, restored the payment of tithes in full.

And I made treasurers over the treasuries, Shelemiah the priest, and Zadok the scribe, and of the Levites, Pedaiah: and next to them was Hanan the son of Zaccur, the son of Mattaniah: for they were counted faithful, and their office was to distribute unto their brethren. Nehemiah appointed faithful stewards over the sacred storehouses, so that the goods would be honestly distributed to the ministers of God.

Remember me, O my God, concerning this, and wipe not out my good deeds that I have done for the house of my God, and for the offices thereof. Nehemiah prays that God would remember his pious works on behalf of the temple, not for vainglory but from holy confidence in God's justice and mercy. # GEM

In those days saw I in Judah some treading wine presses on the sabbath, and bringing in sheaves, and lading asses; as also wine, grapes, and figs, and all manner of burdens, which they brought into Jerusalem on the sabbath day: and I testified against them in the day wherein they sold victuals. Nehemiah now corrects a second abuse: the profanation of the Sabbath by trade and labor.

There dwelt men of Tyre also therein, which brought fish, and all manner of ware, and sold on the sabbath unto the children of Judah, and in Jerusalem. The Tyrians, being Gentiles, observed no Sabbath, and by their trading drew the Jews into violation of the holy day.

Then I contended with the nobles of Judah, and said unto them, What evil thing is this that ye do, and profane the sabbath day? Nehemiah rebukes not the common people only but the nobles, as the chief responsibility for public morality rests with rulers.

Did not your fathers thus, and did not our God bring all this evil upon us, and upon this city? yet ye bring more wrath upon Israel by profaning the sabbath. He argues from history: the violations of the Sabbath by their fathers brought upon them the Babylonian captivity; let them not provoke God again to like punishment.

And it came to pass, that when the gates of Jerusalem began to be dark before the sabbath, I commanded that the gates should be shut, and charged that they should not be opened till after the sabbath: and some of my servants set I at the gates, that there should no burden be brought in on the sabbath day. Nehemiah shut the city gates from the eve of the Sabbath until after it ended, to prevent all commerce, stationing his own servants as guards.
So the merchants and sellers of all kind of ware lodged without Jerusalem once or twice. The merchants camped outside the walls hoping the gates would be opened, but Nehemiah remained firm.

Then I testified against them, and said unto them, Why lodge ye about the wall? if ye do so again, I will lay hands on you. And from that time forth came they no more on the sabbath. Nehemiah threatens the merchants with personal punishment if they persist, and so deters them entirely from coming on the Sabbath.

And I commanded the Levites that they should cleanse themselves, and that they should come and keep the gates, to sanctify the sabbath day. Remember me, O my God, concerning this also, and spare me according to the greatness of thy mercy. Having appointed Levites as Sabbath-keepers at the gates, Nehemiah again commends himself to God's mercy, showing that he sought God's glory, not his own. # GEM

In those days also saw I Jews that had married wives of Ashdod, of Ammon, and of Moab. A third abuse: intermarriage with foreign women, which had been solemnly forbidden and renounced at the time of Esdras.

And their children spake half in the speech of Ashdod, and could not speak in the language of Judah, but according to the language of each people. The corruption of language signified the corruption of religion: children who did not know the Hebrew tongue could not read the Law or worship God as their fathers did.

And I contended with them, and cursed them, and smote certain of them, and plucked off their hair, and made them swear by God, saying, Ye shall not give your daughters unto their sons, nor take their daughters unto your sons, or for yourselves. Nehemiah punished the offenders with blows and the tearing out of their hair, which was a mark of public disgrace, and bound them by oath not to repeat the sin.

Did not Solomon king of Israel sin by these things? yet among many nations was there no king like him, and he was beloved of his God, and God made him king over all Israel: nevertheless even him did outlandish women cause to sin. He cites Solomon as the greatest example of how foreign wives lead even the wisest of men into idolatry, to make the people understand the gravity of the sin. # GEM

Shall we then hearken unto you to do all this great evil, to transgress against our God in marrying strange wives? By citing Solomon's fall, Nehemiah shows that no man should presume on his own wisdom or virtue in the face of this temptation.

And one of the sons of Joiada, the son of Eliashib the high priest, was son in law to Sanballat the Horonite: therefore I chased him from me. Even a grandson of the high priest had contracted a foreign marriage with the daughter of Sanballat the great enemy of Israel; Nehemiah drove him away without mercy, showing that no rank exempts from the law of God.

Remember them, O my God, because they have defiled the priesthood, and the covenant of the priesthood, and of the Levites. Nehemiah prays that God would punish those who defiled the sacred priesthood by foreign marriages, as the priesthood required special purity above all other orders.

Thus cleansed I them from all strangers, and appointed the wards of the priests and the Levites, every one in his business. Nehemiah purified the clergy from all foreign alliances and restored the ancient orders of priestly ministry instituted by David.

And for the wood offering, at times appointed, and for the firstfruits. Remember me, O my God, for good. Nehemiah concludes his memoir with a humble prayer that God would remember all his labors for good, placing his whole confidence not in his own merits but in God's goodness and mercy. # GEM
Thirdly, because Nehemiah here saw Jaddus, who was High Priest and went out to meet Alexander the Great, as

There are those who believe there were two Nehemiahs, separated by a span of two hundred years (for that is the interval from Ezra and Cyrus down to Alexander) and more. But the majority hold that there was one and the same Nehemiah, not two, and this view appears more probable. For Nehemiah, both in this book and in the book [that follows]...
The people gathered as one man in the street before the Water Gate, and they asked Ezra the scribe to bring the book of the Law of Moses, which the Lord had commanded Israel.
Ezra the priest brought the Law before the assembly, both men and women and all who could hear with understanding, on the first day of the seventh month.

He read from it facing the square before the Water Gate from early morning until midday, in the presence of the men and the women and those who could understand; and the ears of all the people were attentive to the book of the Law.

Ezra the scribe stood on a wooden platform which they had made for the purpose; and beside him stood Mattithiah, Shema, Anaiah, Uriah, Hilkiah, and Maaseiah on his right hand; and on his left hand, Pedaiah, Mishael, Malchijah, Hashum, Hash-baddanah, Zechariah and Meshullam.
Ezra opened the book in the sight of all the people, for he was above all the people; and when he opened it, all the people stood up.
Then Ezra blessed the Lord, the great God, and all the people answered, Amen, Amen, lifting up their hands; and they bowed their heads and worshiped the Lord with their faces to the ground.

Also Jeshua, Bani, Sherebiah, Jamin, Akkub, Shabbethai, Hodiah, Maaseiah, Kelita, Azariah, Jozabad, Hanan, Pelaiah, the Levites, helped the people to understand the Law, while the people remained in their places.

They read from the book, from the Law of God, clearly; and they gave the sense, so that the people understood the reading. # GEM Lapide notes that this verse establishes the apostolic tradition of expounding Scripture with authoritative interpretation, not merely reading the bare text, so that the faithful understand its true meaning — a practice the Church has always maintained against those who would leave Scripture without a guide.

And Nehemiah, who was the governor, and Ezra the priest and scribe, and the Levites who taught the people said to all the people, This day is holy to the Lord your God; do not mourn or weep. For all the people wept when they heard the words of the Law.

Then he said to them, Go your way, eat the fat and drink sweet wine and send portions to him for whom nothing is prepared; for this day is holy to our Lord; and do not be grieved, for the joy of the Lord is your strength. # GEM Lapide: "The joy of the Lord is your strength" — that is, spiritual joy born of faith and the hearing of God's word fortifies the soul against all adversity, far more than any bodily comfort; whence Bernard says that holy joy is the foretaste of heavenly beatitude.
So the Levites quieted all the people, saying, Be still, for this day is holy; do not be grieved.
And all the people went their way to eat and drink and to send portions and to make great rejoicing, because they had understood the words that were declared to them.
On the second day the heads of fathers' houses of all the people, with the priests and the Levites, came together to Ezra the scribe in order to study the words of the Law.
And they found it written in the Law that the Lord had commanded by Moses that the people of Israel should dwell in booths during the feast of the seventh month,

and that they should publish and proclaim in all their towns and in Jerusalem, Go out to the hills and bring branches of olive, wild olive, myrtle, palm, and other leafy trees to make booths, as it is written.

So the people went out and brought them and made booths for themselves, each on his roof, and in their courts and in the courts of the house of God, and in the square at the Water Gate and in the square at the Gate of Ephraim.

And all the assembly of those who had returned from the captivity made booths and lived in the booths; for from the days of Jeshua the son of Nun to that day the people of Israel had not done so. And there was very great rejoicing.

And day by day, from the first day to the last day, he read from the book of the Law of God. They kept the feast seven days; and on the eighth day there was a solemn assembly, according to the ordinance.

That is, that he had commanded that priests of uncertain and doubtful [lineage] should not eat of the consecrated offerings, until it should be established by the oracle of the high priest that they were true priests; the very same thing [is said] here.

Nehemiah and Joshua (or Jesus son of Josedec) came to Jerusalem under Cyrus; fourthly, because in chapters 8 and 9, Nehemiah together with Ezra interpreted the law to the people. Therefore Nehemiah was one person and the same companion of Ezra, yet younger than him. So hold Salianus, Sanchez, and others.

To the first objection the reply is that Nehemiah was long-lived, and therefore preserved strong and vigorous by God into great old age, for the benefit of the people, so that he might rebuild Jerusalem and plead the cause of the citizens before Artaxerxes. So too Ezra lived 132 years and more, as I showed in the preface to Ezra.
To the second objection the reply is that Nehemiah is the author of this book, and therefore he sometimes speaks of himself in the first person, and not always; for in the third person

Allegor. Nehemiah was a type of Christ both in name and in reality: for Nehemiah in Hebrew means the same as "consoler of God"; because he himself was sent by God for the consolation of the people, that he might strengthen them in rebuilding Jerusalem. So Christ was sent by the Father in the flesh, that he might console those who mourn in

Nehemiah, having received a sign from God—both because he had made King Artaxerxes favorably disposed and generous toward him, and because God had prospered his journey through so many enemies and difficulties, overcoming them—therefore Nehemiah, signifying to the Jews both God's and the king's goodwill toward himself and them, encouraged them to resume generously and fervently the construction of the city walls, which had been abandoned or negligently attended to on account of the attacks of the Samaritans.

[And Sanballat the Horonite heard.] This Sanballat is different from the Sanballat who was the father-in-law of Manasseh, the first High Priest of the Samaritans on Mount Gerizim. For that Horonite preceded this father-in-law of Manasseh by 109 years, says Salianus, of whom more will be said below.

[And Tobiah the servant, the Ammonite.] He had been raised from the condition of a slave and bondsman, and had been appointed governor of Ammonitis by the king of the Persians, just as Sanballat over Samaria and Goshem over Arabia. These three governors of neighboring regions were therefore hostile to Nehemiah and the Jews, and adversaries in the building work.

[But you have no part, and right, and memorial in Jerusalem.] 'Justice,' that is, 'right'—as if to say: You, who are like Samaritans (once transported by the Assyrians to inhabit Samaria, 3 Kings 17), Ammonites and Arabs, have received authority from the Persians to administer Samaria, Ammonitis, and Arabia, but not Judaea and Jerusalem, nor have you ever dwelt in Jerusalem; for there is no memory of you in it. Therefore attend to your own affairs among those committed to your charge; but do not meddle in our affairs, for I have received full authority from the king of the Persians to rebuild Jerusalem: beware therefore lest you oppose me, for otherwise you will be opposing the king of the Persians and will find him your avenger. So Cajetan, Sanchez, and others.
SYNOPSIS OF CHAPTER 3: NEHEMIAH RECORDS WHAT PART OF THE WALL, WHAT TOWERS AND GATES, EACH PERSON REBUILT.

[And Eliashib the high priest rose up, and his brethren the priests, and they built the sheep gate; they sanctified it and set up its doors, and even unto the tower of a hundred cubits they sanctified it, unto the tower of Hananeel.] Eliashib the high priest led by example, rousing the others to the work by his own initiative and authority. It was fitting that the priests should begin with the sheep gate, since through it the animals destined for sacrifice were led into the Temple; hence they sanctified it with special rites, as befitting a sacred entrance connected with divine worship.
[And next to him the men of Jericho built; and next to him Zaccur the son of Imri built.]
[But the fish gate the sons of Hassenaah built; they roofed it and set up its doors and bolts and bars. And next to them Meremoth the son of Uriah, the son of Hakkoz, built.]
[And next to him Meshullam the son of Berechiah, the son of Meshezabeel, built; and next to them Zadok the son of Baana built.]

[And next to them the Tekoites built; but their nobles did not put their necks to the work of their Lord.] # GEM The nobles of the Tekoites are here censured by name, to their perpetual shame, because they refused to submit their necks to the work—that is, they scorned to stoop to labor they deemed beneath their dignity. Pride and the love of ease made them despise a holy and necessary work. Their common people, however, did not refuse. Thus the lowly often surpass the great in zeal for God's house, as the poor widow surpassed the rich in the Temple treasury.
[And the old gate Jehoiada the son of Paseah and Meshullam the son of Besodeiah repaired; they roofed it and set up its doors and bolts and bars.]
[And next to them Melatiah the Gibeonite and Jadon the Meronothite repaired, men of Gibeon and Mizpah, under the jurisdiction of the governor who was beyond the River.]

[And next to him Uzziel the son of Harhaiah, one of the goldsmiths, repaired; and next to him Hananiah the son of one of the apothecaries repaired; and they left Jerusalem unto the broad wall.] Note that craftsmen—goldsmiths and apothecaries—contributed to the sacred work of rebuilding, each repairing the section adjacent to his own district. God accepts the labor of every trade and condition when offered in His service.
[And next to him Rephaiah the son of Hur, the ruler of half the district of Jerusalem, repaired.]
[And next to him...]

In the dedication of the wall of Jerusalem, as it is said in v. 27: "In the dedication of the wall of Jerusalem they sought out the Levites." And in v. 30: "And the priests and Levites purified themselves, and purified the people, and the gates, and the wall." The Gentiles imitated the same practice. Hence Servius, in his commentary on Book 3 of the Aeneid, records that they sacrificed to Father Liber at the founding of a city, so that the city might be free. Hear Aeneas, a fugitive from Troy, planning a new city in Thrace, Aeneid 3, shortly after the beginning: "I set the first walls upon the site, entering under evil fates, and frame the name Aeneadae from my own name. I was offering sacred rites to Dione's mother and to the gods as patrons of my undertaking; and I was slaughtering a gleaming bull on the shore to the heavenly king of the gods above." Moreover, among the Gentiles, gates and walls were sacred, as Alexander ab Alexandro teaches in Book 6 of the Geniales Dies, ch. 49, and Tiraquellus there.
[UNTO THE TOWER OF THE HUNDRED CUBITS.] Heb. unto the tower of Meah, which was called Meah, that is, one hundred, because it was one hundred cubits high.

[AND THE OLD GATE JOIADA AND OTHERS BUILT.] Note from Villalpando, vol. 3, book 3, ch. 26, that the principal gates of Jerusalem were twelve, which were situated on three sides of the city, facing three quarters of the world; for each side had four gates. On the fourth side, facing south, where the sheer cliffs of Mount Zion enclosed the city and denied exit and entrance, there was no gate. The eastern side therefore had, first, the Sheep Gate already mentioned; second, the Horse Gate, through which horses were led to water at the brook Kedron; third, the Water Gate, so called from the gathering of the waters of the fountain of Siloah, which Hezekiah made there; fourth, the Potsherd Gate, because potters practiced their craft near it and formed earthen vessels.

The northern side of the city had, first, the Gate of Benjamin, because through it one went into the territory of the tribe of Benjamin; second, the Fish Gate, through which fish were brought into the city and the fish market; third, the Judicial Gate, in which judges sat and exercised judgment over both strangers and citizens, which was near the temple and next to the Sheep Gate. This same gate is called in this verse the Old Gate, because in it there remained fabric of the old Jebusite construction.
Sanballat, hearing that they were building the wall, was greatly angered and exceedingly moved, and he mocked the Jews.

He spoke before his brethren and the assembly of the Samaritans: "What are the feeble Jews doing? Will the nations leave them alone? Will they sacrifice? Will they complete it in one day? Can they revive the stones out of the heaps of rubbish, which are burned?"
Tobias the Ammonite, his companion, added: "Let them build; if a fox goes up, he will break down their stone wall."
Nehemiah prays: "Hear, O our God, for we are despised; turn back their reproach upon their own head, and give them over as a prey in a land of captivity."
Cover not their iniquity, and let not their sin be blotted out from before thee, for they have provoked thee to anger before the builders. # GEM
So they built the wall, and joined it together.
SYNOPSIS: The Jews build with one hand and hold a sword in the other to repel the attacking Samaritans; at the urging and prayer of Nehemiah, the building of the walls and gates is completed.

Concerning verse 8: they left off the building of Jerusalem as far as the wall of the broad place, that is, they left off the building of the wall for a space of 400 cubits between the gate of Ephraim and the gate of the corner already mentioned, because not long before King Azariah had excellently repaired and fortified this side so that it could not be thrown down by the Chaldeans.

Unto the house of the mighty men — in which had dwelt the thirty most valiant heroes of David and afterwards their successors. For this house seems to have been a kind of seminary or college of strong men, who handled arms, led armies, and taught soldiers to fight. See what was said at 2 Kings 23:8 and following.

After him Hasediah, ruler of half the district of Keilah, built in his own district. Jerusalem was distributed into its own districts, and over each district presided its own leader and prefect, who is here called a prince. The same arrangement still exists at Rome. For Rome from of old was divided into its own districts or regions, each of which had its own prefect, called in Italian Caporioni, that is, heads of the regions of the city, who when the see is vacant hold great authority; for it is their duty to protect the city and suppress tumults, and accordingly to punish offenders.
Unto the house of the Nethinim — these were the Gibeonites, concerning whom I have spoken at Joshua 9.

Regarding the twelve gates of Jerusalem: the northern side had first the sheep gate, second the fish gate, third the old gate following the traces of Melchisedech, fourth the gate of Ephraim through which one went to the tribe of Ephraim. This fourth gate and the next, the gate of the corner, are not named here because they did not need restoration, as is intimated at verse 8. The western side had first the gate of the corner, so called because it stood at the western and northern corner of the city; second the gate of the valley of corpses and Calvary; third the dung gate; fourth the fountain gate, concerning which I have spoken in the preceding chapter at verse 13. To these twelve gates of Jerusalem St. John alludes, Apocalypse 21:12, where he enumerates the same number of gates of the heavenly Jerusalem.

Shall the nations permit them? — as if to say: shall we, the neighboring nations, so many and so powerful, allow the Jews to build their Jerusalem, from which they might then invade and harass us as they once did? As if to say: By no means; in no way ought we to permit them to build this citadel and place it upon our shoulders.

Shall they sacrifice and complete it in one day? — as if to say: Do they think that in one day, that is, in a very short time, they can both begin the construction of so great a city (which would be done by offering sacrifices and dedicating the walls, and thereupon feasting) and also complete it? As if to say: By no means; for we shall intervene in time, and disturb them, and by force compel them to cease from building.

Can they revive the stones from the heaps of rubbish, which are burned? — The Hebrew for 'build' is 'vivify'; the Septuagint has 'heal.' As if to say: Can the Jews reform the bricks and stones burned by the Chaldeans and reduced to ashes, and restore them to their former solidity, strength, and usefulness as stones and bricks, so as to rebuild the walls from them? As if to say: The Jews attempt a most difficult thing, indeed an impossible construction.

If a fox go up, he shall break down their stone wall. — Hebrew: a fox will go up and break through their stone wall. This is a sarcasm or hostile mockery, as if to say: Let the Jews do, labor, and sweat as much as they wish — so fragile and low will be the wall they erect that a fox could leap over it, indeed demolish it. So Cajetan.

Cover not their iniquity — with oblivion and impunity. As if to say: Remember, O Lord, these wrongs and taunts with which our enemies unjustly afflict us, and restrain and punish them. For when God spares, He is said to cover sins; when He does not spare but punishes, He is said to uncover, behold, and set them before His eyes. Hence St. Stephen praying for the Jews said: 'Lord, lay not this sin to their charge,' Acts 7. Therefore Nehemiah does not pray that God should not forgive the guilt of the penitent — for to ask this would be impious and diabolical — but that He should not remit the punishment of the impenitent who persist in their sin.

And Judah said — that is, the Jews said, who, wearied by labor and terrified by the threats of enemies, began to grow faint in the work and to murmur against Nehemiah who was pressing on with the building.

The strength of the bearers of burdens is decayed — that is, the shoulders and hands of the workmen and carriers, who bore stones, timber, water, and lime, have been weakened by so great, so constant, and so prolonged labor, that they can scarcely carry those burdens any further.

And there is much rubbish — as if to say: there is need of much lime, earth, or, as in Hebrew and Greek, of crushed and mixed dust. Or more simply: the earth, that is, in excavating the ground and laying the foundations of the walls, so very much debris of earth and stones remains to be cleared away and carried off that it cannot be removed and transported by the few workmen and carriers. Nevertheless Nehemiah's gentleness in bearing with them and his constancy in encouraging them overcame even this complaint of his people.
Saying to us ten times — that is, many times they told and reported to us the ambushes of the enemies and their imminent secret attacks.

From all places — as if to say: informants came from everywhere reporting the same thing, namely that the enemies were planning to attack us by ambush. Therefore Nehemiah, arming his men and drawing them up in battle order, forestalled, dispersed, and averted the enemies' plots.

Of those who built — (supply: every one) with one hand wrought in the work, and with the other held a sword — so as to repel enemies rushing suddenly from ambush. Note here the prudence as well as the constancy and zeal of the people in building. Thus in former times the Spaniards, fighting continually with the Saracens who occupied Spain, whenever they prayed or did anything else, kept a sword at hand to drive off those who attacked them; whence the custom still prevails that even now the country-folk in Spain go about armed with swords, indeed do not go out unless girded with a sword. So even now the Hungarians leap from the plough to arms when they see the Turks suddenly invading them.

# GEM For the allegory and tropology, see Bede, who says: "For the builders are girded about the loins with a sword when those who persist in good works and take care to order those committed to them with disciplined reason (for this is to place living stones in the building of the Church in fitting order) strive to restrain in themselves the flowing sharpness of lust with the sword of the word of God." Therefore, while we are engaged in reason, in works of charity, in preaching, and in other pious acts of virtue, by which we build the spiritual edifice of merits in the soul, let us hold in the other hand the sword of mortification, with which to subdue the temptations of the flesh.

S. DOMOS NOSTRAS OPPONAMUS.] That is, let us pledge them to moneylenders. For they had recently returned from Babylon and had consumed all their resources on the journey and on building the city. Wherefore they labored under great poverty, to relieve which they contemplated pledging their possessions, indeed even selling their sons and daughters into servitude (v. 2).
IN TRIBUTIS REGIS.] That is, so that we may obtain what we are obliged to pay annually to the king of Persia in place of tribute.

ET NUNC SICUT CARNES FRATRUM NOSTRORUM, SIC CARNES NOSTRAE SUNT; ET SICUT FILII EORUM, ITA ET FILII NOSTRI.] As if to say: both we and our children serve this hard servitude similar to that which our brethren serve — that is, our fellow tribesmen who remained in Babylon and Persia — and this through the iniquity and injustice of the wealthier Jews, who oppress us, their poor brethren, with usury and the purchase of children, as follows. Wherefore we are in a worse condition than our brethren in Persia: for those are afflicted by Persians, who are foreigners and idolaters; but we are afflicted by our own fellow tribesmen, who are our brethren in blood and in religion. For it is more bitter to be tormented by a friend and brother than by an enemy and stranger. For since the law in Deuteronomy 23:19 commands that a loan be given to brethren freely, they exact great usury from us for a loan; wherefore we are compelled to sell or deliver our sons and daughters into servitude in order to pay this usury. So Cajetan, Vatablus, and others.
CONGREGAVI ADVERSUM EOS (the wealthy usurers) CONCLONEM MAGNAM.] That is, a great assembly of the whole people, so that, reproved and put to shame before it by me, they might remit their usury.

CENTESIMAM PECUNIAE, FRUMENTI, VINI ET OLEI.] The hundredth, namely the usury, whereby they exacted monthly for the use of a loan, i.e., as usury, one per hundred — that is, twelve per hundred annually. Hence it is clear that this usury was common among the Jews, as it was later among the Romans; and it was heavy, inasmuch as in it every year an eighth part of the principal sum borrowed was paid; for twelve gold pieces are, for example, an eighth part of a hundred gold pieces, because twelve taken a hundred times makes 96, to which add four and you have a hundred. Wherefore in the eighth year the usury nearly equaled the principal itself, and thus nearly doubled it, so that for a loan of a hundred gold pieces nearly two hundred gold pieces were owed, precisely 196; and after another eight years it tripled, and after yet another eight it quadrupled. But when half of this usury, namely six per hundred annually, was paid, it was called usura semissis, or the halved usury; when four per hundred was paid, it was called trientaria; when only one, it was called uncialis, which was the minimum usury, just as the duodenaria was the maximum.

DATE PRO ILLIS.] That is, forgive it to them; indeed also return what you have received and restore it, as preceded: whence these words are not found in the Hebrew, because they are to be understood as repeated from the preceding. Since usury is against commutative justice, it does not pass into the ownership of the usurer; hence it must be restored by him to the one who gave it as payment for the loan.

ET DIXERUNT.] Moved partly by shame, partly by the authority and example of Nehemiah, partly by love of the common good, partly by compassion for the poor: REDDEMUS — we will restore the fields, vineyards, and olive groves which the poor had pledged to us as security for loans received, handing them over as pledges until they repaid the loan — ET AB EIS NIHIL QUAEREMUS — that is, we will exact nothing as usury. ET VOCAVI SACERDOTES (guilty of this usury), and (privately out of reverence for the priesthood, lest I put them to shame before the people) ADJURAVI EOS, that they should act according to what I had said.

From the day the king commanded me to be governor in the land of Judah, from the twentieth year to the thirty-second year of Artaxerxes—twelve years—I and my brothers did not eat the food allowance that was due to the governors. The governors were accustomed to receive from the citizens of Jerusalem, over whom they presided, provisions (annona), that is, necessaries for food—a fixed measure of grain, wine, oil, money, and the like. But Nehemiah was unwilling to accept these, both lest he burden the poor people with this contribution, and also to give an example to the wealthy of remitting usury. And he did this for twelve years, during which he presided over Jerusalem as governor. Hence many think he remained continuously in Jerusalem for twelve years. Yet this does not fully satisfy the desire of the king and queen, who wished Nehemiah to return to them promptly, and with a short time appointed by him, as has been said.

He appointed Hanani his brother (or kinsman) - this is that Hanani who brought news of the desolation of Jerusalem to Nehemiah in Persia, ch. 1 v. 2 - and Hananiah the prince (that is, prefect) of the citadel, namely of the royal palace, as the Hebrews have it. These two, as men above all others truthful, that is, sincere and upright, and fearing God, Nehemiah set as guardians of the city of Jerusalem.

While they were still standing by - that is, not unless these two guardians, namely Hanani and Hananiah, were present and standing by were the gates shut in the evening, lest any treachery arise from the ambushes of enemies. Again in the morning they were not opened before the heat of the sun, that is, before broad daylight, when all are awake.

And the people were few - who had returned in small numbers from Babylon; and from these few, some seeing the desolation of the city and the threats of enemies had betaken themselves to other cities and villages of Judea, while others had returned to Babylon or Persia, among whom was the leader Zerubbabel.

But God put it into my heart - as if to say: God suggested to me the counsel and means of supplying the scarcity of inhabitants of Jerusalem, namely by reviewing the catalogue of those who had first returned from Babylon with Ezra, and in accordance with it, by inviting and summoning them to inhabit Jerusalem, which we shall hear was actually done by lot in ch. 11. Hence he presently reviews the catalogue containing the census of the people who had returned.

These are the sons of the province who went up from the captivity of the migration - that is, who returned from the migration of Babylon, first from Babylon to Jerusalem with Zerubbabel under Cyrus. This is the catalogue or census of the people which Ezra reviewed in ch. 2, but in different words and numbers for the most part, especially in the middle; for the beginning and end are the same in both places. The reason is that this census of the people or catalogue was undertaken and recorded several times: first in Babylon, when the Jews departed thence; second in Jerusalem, when they first arrived there; third when, the temple having been rebuilt, it was dedicated with a solemn gathering of the people. Now these censuses varied among themselves, because as some died others were born, as some departed others arrived. Therefore Ezra drew his catalogue from one census, Nehemiah from another; and hence they differ. See Francis Lucas in note 79.
Men of Beeroth, seven hundred forty-three.
Men of Ramah and Geba, six hundred twenty-one.
Men of Michmas, one hundred twenty-two.
Men of Bethel and Ai, one hundred twenty-three.
Men of the other Nebo, fifty-two.
Men of the other Elam, one thousand two hundred fifty-four.
Sons of Harim, three hundred twenty.
Sons of Jericho, three hundred forty-five.
Sons of Lod, Hadid, and Ono, seven hundred twenty-one.
Sons of Senaah, three thousand nine hundred thirty.
Priests: sons of Jedaiah in the house of Jeshua, nine hundred seventy-three.
Sons of Immer, one thousand fifty-two.
Sons of Pashhur, one thousand two hundred forty-seven.
Sons of Harim, one thousand seventeen.
Levites: sons of Jeshua and Kadmiel of the sons of Hodaviah, seventy-four.
Singers: sons of Asaph, one hundred forty-eight.
Gatekeepers: sons of Shallum, sons of Ater, sons of Talmon, sons of Akkub, sons of Hatita, sons of Shobai, one hundred thirty-eight.
Nethinim: sons of Ziha, sons of Hasupha, sons of Tabbaoth,
sons of Keros, sons of Sia, sons of Padon, sons of Lebana, sons of Hagaba, sons of Shalmai,
sons of Hanan, sons of Giddel, sons of Gahar,
sons of Reaiah, sons of Rezin, sons of Nekoda,
sons of Gazzam, sons of Uzza, sons of Paseah,
sons of Besai, sons of Meunim, sons of Nephushesim,
sons of Bakbuk, sons of Hakupha, sons of Harhur,
sons of Bazlith, sons of Mehida, sons of Harsha,
sons of Barkos, sons of Sisera, sons of Temah,
sons of Neziah, sons of Hatipha,
sons of Solomon's servants: sons of Sotai, sons of Sophereth, sons of Perida,
sons of Jaala, sons of Darkon, sons of Giddel,
sons of Shephatiah, sons of Hattil, sons of Pochereth-hazzebaim, sons of Amon.
All the Nethinim and sons of Solomon's servants, three hundred ninety-two.
These are they who went up from Tel-melah, Tel-harsha, Cherub, Addon, and Immer; and they could not show their fathers' house nor their descent, whether they were of Israel.
Sons of Delaiah, sons of Tobiah, sons of Nekoda, six hundred forty-two.
And of the priests: sons of Habaiah, sons of Hakkoz, sons of Barzillai, who took a wife of the daughters of Barzillai the Gileadite and was called by their name.
These sought their registration among those enrolled in the genealogies, but it was not found; so they were excluded from the priesthood.
And the Tirshatha said to them that they should not eat of the most holy things until a priest stood up with Urim and Thummim - that is, a priest learned and instructed.
And the Tirshatha - this is Nehemiah, as is clear from ch. 8 v. 9.
The whole assembly together was forty-two thousand three hundred sixty,
besides their male and female servants, who were seven thousand three hundred thirty-seven; and among them were male and female singers, two hundred forty-five.
Their horses, seven hundred thirty-six; their mules, two hundred forty-five;
their camels, four hundred thirty-five; their donkeys, six thousand seven hundred twenty.
Now some of the heads of fathers' houses gave to the work. The Tirshatha gave into the treasury: gold, one thousand drachmas; fifty basins; five hundred thirty priestly garments.
And some of the heads of fathers' houses gave into the treasury of the work: gold, twenty thousand drachmas; and silver, two thousand two hundred minas.
And what the rest of the people gave: gold, twenty thousand drachmas; silver, two thousand minas; and priestly garments, sixty-seven.
So the priests and the Levites and the gatekeepers and the singers and some of the people and the Nethinim and all Israel lived in their cities.

The seventh month had come, and all the people gathered as one man at the plaza before the Water Gate, asking Ezra the scribe to bring the book of the Law of Moses which the Lord had commanded Israel.
Ezra the priest brought the Law before the assembly of men and women and all who could understand, on the first day of the seventh month.
He read from it openly in the plaza before the Water Gate from morning until midday, before men and women and the learned; and the ears of all the people were attentive to the Book.

Ezra the scribe stood upon a wooden platform which he had made for speaking; and beside him stood Mattithiah, Shema, Anaiah, Uriah, Hilkiah, and Maaseiah on his right hand; and on his left, Pedaiah, Mishael, Malchijah, Hashum, Hashbaddanah, Zechariah, and Meshullam.
Ezra opened the book before all the people, for he was elevated above all the people; and when he opened it, all the people stood.
Ezra blessed the Lord the great God, and all the people answered: Amen, Amen, lifting up their hands; and they bowed down and worshipped God, prostrate upon the ground. # GEM

Now Jeshua, Bani, Sherebiah, Jamin, Akkub, Shabbethai, Hodiah, Maaseiah, Kelita, Azariah, Jozabad, Hanan, Pelaiah — the Levites — made silence among the people to hear the Law; and the people stood in their place.
They read in the Book of the Law of God distinctly and openly so as to give understanding; and they understood when it was read.

Then Nehemiah (he is the Tirshatha) and Ezra the priest and scribe, and the Levites who interpreted to all the people, said: This day is holy to the Lord our God; do not mourn and do not weep. For all the people wept when they heard the words of the Law.

He said to them: Go, eat rich food and drink sweet wine, and send portions to those who have prepared nothing for themselves; for this is a holy day of the Lord, and do not be grieved: for the joy of the Lord is our strength. # GEM
The Levites made silence throughout all the people, saying: Be still, for the day is holy, and do not grieve.
And all the people went away to eat and drink.

Synopsis of Chapter Eight: Ezra, having convened all the people on the first day of the seventh month, publicly reads and expounds the Law of God. He does the same on the fifteenth day of the month throughout the eight days of the Feast of Tabernacles, which is celebrated by all with great solemnity.

On the catalog of donations in the preceding chapter: this list of gifts offered for the building is the same as that reviewed by Ezra in chapter 2, but differs in numbers, perhaps because Ezra encompassed the gifts of more donors — including all who, under Cyrus's edict, contributed something from their own resources for the building or later sent contributions to Jerusalem — whereas Nehemiah records only the gifts of those returning from captivity, and only the principal ones, and therefore fewer than Ezra, as Francis Lucas notes.

On the interpolated passage omitted by some interpreters: Lyra and Cajetan judge it should be omitted and indeed reject it. The markedly different character of the passage sufficiently argues that these words were inserted and are neither from the text nor of faith, says Sanchez. Nevertheless the Rome-corrected Bible retains this annotation. It therefore seems to have been added by the Translator, as many similar additions were made in the book of Esther, or by some paraphrast or commentator; so Francis Lucas notes.

These events seem to have taken place in the seventh year of Artaxerxes under Ezra, who was sent by him and returned to Jerusalem. Hence just as the catalogue of the preceding chapter of Nehemiah is the same
and drink, and send portions, and make great joy: because they had understood the words which had been taught them.
And on the second day the chief men of the families of all the people, the priests and the Levites, were gathered together to Ezra the scribe, that he should interpret to them the words of the Law.
And they found written in the Law, that the Lord had commanded by the hand of Moses, that the children of Israel should dwell in tabernacles on the solemn feast of the seventh month:

and that they should proclaim and publish the voice in all their cities and in Jerusalem, saying: Go forth to the mount, and bring branches of olive, and branches of the most beautiful tree, branches of myrtle, and branches of palms, and branches of thick trees, to make tabernacles, as it is written.

And the people went forth and brought them. And they made themselves tabernacles, every man upon the roof of his house, and in their courts, and in the courts of the house of God, and in the street of the water gate, and in the street of the gate of Ephraim.

And all the assembly of those that had returned from captivity made tabernacles, and dwelt in tabernacles: for since the days of Josue the son of Nun the children of Israel had not done so until that day. And there was exceeding great joy.
Also he read in the book of the law of God day by day, from the first day to the last day, and they kept the solemnity seven days, and on the eighth day a solemn assembly according to the manner.

In the month of Nisan, in the twentieth year of King Artaxerxes — Nehemiah had been cupbearer to the king for some time, and now for the first time showed sadness before him. For previously he had concealed his grief, lest the king be offended by a sorrowful countenance; now however, moved by divine inspiration, he showed his sorrow, so that he might obtain leave to rebuild Jerusalem.

The month of Nisan corresponds to our March-April. This was the twentieth year of Artaxerxes Longimanus, which is the same king mentioned in Ezra. Some reckon this the first year of the seventy weeks of Daniel, from which the coming of Christ is to be computed.

The king noticed Nehemiah's sadness, for sorrow of heart shows itself in the face. A sad countenance betrays an afflicted mind. Nehemiah was afraid because it was dangerous to appear sad before kings, who wish all things to be cheerful and prosperous in their presence.

Nehemiah wisely does not immediately reveal the true cause of his sorrow, but speaks in general terms: "Why should my face not be sad, when the city of my fathers' sepulchres lies waste?" He mentions the sepulchres of his fathers to move the king to pity, for even pagans held the tombs of ancestors in great reverence.

The king asks what Nehemiah requests. Before answering, Nehemiah prays silently to God — a brief but fervent prayer, as Chrysostom notes. # GEM This teaches us that we should invoke God's help before undertaking any important matter, even when time is short.

Nehemiah asks permission to go to Judah to rebuild Jerusalem. He speaks with great modesty: "if it please the king," showing that he does not demand but humbly petitions. He also offers to return, signifying his continued loyalty to the king.

The queen sitting beside the king — Josephus says this was Damaspia, the wife of Artaxerxes. Her presence perhaps made the king more gracious, as queens are often more compassionate. The king sets a time for Nehemiah's journey, and Nehemiah prudently asks for a definite term so that he might complete the work.

Nehemiah asks for letters to the governors beyond the river, that they might give him safe passage. This shows prudence: he who undertakes a great work must seek the necessary means. He also asks for letters to Asaph, keeper of the king's forest, for timber to build the gates and walls.

God's good hand was upon Nehemiah — that is, divine providence and grace assisted him, so that the king granted all his requests. Whatever good we obtain from rulers or from any source comes ultimately from God's gracious hand. # GEM

Nehemiah comes to the governors beyond the river and delivers the king's letters. The king also sent captains of the army and horsemen with him, for his protection and to lend authority to his mission.

Sanballat the Horonite and Tobiah the Ammonite were greatly displeased that someone had come to seek the welfare of the children of Israel. These men were enemies of the Jews, envious of their restoration. Sanballat was perhaps from Horonaim in Moab; Tobiah was an Ammonite. Envy always grieves at the good fortune of others. # GEM
Nehemiah arrives at Jerusalem and remains there three days — resting, preparing himself, and quietly observing the situation before revealing his purpose to anyone.

Nehemiah rises at night with a few trusted men to inspect the walls secretly. He reveals his plan to no one, lest enemies obstruct the work before it begins. Prudence demands secrecy in great undertakings, as even Christ commanded silence in certain matters.

Nehemiah goes out by night and inspects the walls and gates, finding them broken down and burned. He surveys the full extent of the ruin, so that he might plan the repair comprehensively. Good leaders first carefully examine the magnitude of a task before proposing remedies.

The gate of the fountain and the king's pool — these are specific places in Jerusalem whose exact locations are disputed among commentators. Nehemiah finds the way so blocked by rubble that his beast cannot pass, showing how great the destruction was.
He goes up through the valley by night and views the wall, then returns and enters by the valley gate. This nocturnal inspection was done secretly to avoid exciting the opposition of enemies.

The rulers did not know where Nehemiah had gone or what he was doing, for he had revealed his purpose to no one as yet — neither to Jews, priests, nobles, magistrates, nor any workers. Silence and prudent secrecy are virtues in those who undertake great works for God. # GEM

Now Nehemiah reveals his plan to the leaders: "You see the evil in which we are — Jerusalem is desolate and its gates burned. Come, let us build the wall." He first sets before them the misery of the situation to stir them to action, then proposes the remedy. Good leaders inspire others by showing both the need and the possibility of remedy.

Nehemiah tells them of God's good hand upon him and the king's favorable words. He attributes success not to his own merits but to God's grace, thereby encouraging the people that God himself supports this work. They resolve with one accord: "Let us rise up and build." # GEM

Sanballat, Tobiah, and Geshem the Arabian mock and despise the Jews, asking whether they are rebelling against the king. This is the tactic of enemies: to discourage by mockery and false accusations. Mockery is a weapon of the weak against the strong.

Nehemiah's noble response: "The God of heaven, he will prosper us, and we his servants will arise and build; but you have no portion, nor right, nor memorial in Jerusalem." He appeals to divine authority and firmly excludes the enemies from any share in the holy work. Those who do not belong to God's people have no claim upon his holy city. # GEM

[Introduction] Both are named: the seventh month, Ezra, the feast of Tabernacles, etc. Nor is there any doubt that Ezra the scribe, having discharged his duty and zeal, as soon as he returned to Jerusalem in the seventh year of Artaxerxes, taught the people who had forgotten the law in captivity. For he was sent for this purpose by Artaxerxes, who said that Ezra should freely teach the ignorant (Ezra 7:25), just as Zerubbabel and Jeshua were sent to rebuild the temple, while Nehemiah was sent to restore the walls and the city. Add to this: Ezra does not appear to have lived until the twentieth year of Artaxerxes, when Nehemiah restored the walls; for then he would have been 147 years old (which was unheard of in that age): for so many years elapsed from the last year of Zedekiah, in which Seraiah, the father of Ezra, was slain, until the twentieth year of Artaxerxes. Yet this reasoning is not solid: for that Ezra lived in the 20th year of Artaxerxes is clear from ch. 12, vv. 33 and 35, where Ezra the scribe is said to have been present at the dedication of the city made by Nehemiah; though Cajetan thinks that there another Ezra is meant, different from our own. Wherefore some hold that these things occurred under Nehemiah in the 20th year of Artaxerxes, so that Ezra returned with him a third time to Jerusalem, and was of great age, namely about 110 years.
AND EZRA THE SCRIBE STOOD UPON A PULPIT OF WOOD. [Heb. upon a wooden tower.] That is, upon a platform which rose up from the ground after the manner of a small tower.

And all the people stood, both that they might show reverence to God by standing while the law was read, and that they might the more easily catch the reading of Ezra with attentive ears while standing in silence. Hence also Christians rise in reverence at the Gospel. # GEM

And they read (both Ezra and his companions, who took turns in reading when Ezra was old and weary; but when any difficulty arose, Ezra himself explained it) in the book of the law (in the Pentateuch, and especially in Deuteronomy, where the whole law is repeated and reiterated) distinctly, so that all might perfectly hear each part. The Septuagint renders: And he distinguished in the knowledge of the Lord, that is, he read and expounded the law distinctly through the knowledge received from the Lord. Some therefore wrongly twist this to the restoration of books burned by the Chaldeans, as though Ezra had restored them from memory through knowledge infused by God. AND TO GIVE THE SENSE [Heb. putting, i.e. giving, understanding]: meaning that Ezra while reading explained where necessary in Syriac or Chaldean what was read in Hebrew from Deuteronomy; and moreover interpreted paraphrastically the sentences that occurred, as is said in v. 13. For the people had corrupted their Hebrew tongue in Babylon and mixed it with Chaldean; wherefore they did not sufficiently understand pure Hebrew. Ezra therefore interpreted what was said in Hebrew into the Syriac language, which was by then the people's own native tongue upon their return.

AND NEHEMIAH SAID (HE IS THE ATHERSATHA). Nehemiah is called Athersatha from the root ירש [yaras], that is, he inherited; as if to say Athersatha, that is, אתה ירשת [attah yarashta], meaning: thou alone above all others hast possessed the inheritance of the fathers, since thou hast restored the ruined city, surrounded it with walls, and handed it down safe to thy posterity to possess. So Vitalpandus. Or from תירוש [tirosh], meaning new wine, and שתה [shata], meaning he drank; as if to say: pre-taster of the king's wine, for he was cupbearer. So Marinus in the Lexicon. Thirdly, Athersatha closely corresponds with Artaxerxes. For it seems that the king granted Nehemiah the use of his own name, or certainly Nehemiah himself, being most dear to the king, was addressed by the king's own name by the courtiers. So Vitalpandus, Part 1, De Templo.

Furthermore, what Lyranus says seems rather fanciful — that "Athersatha" in Hebrew means the same as "released," and that Nehemiah is so called because he was dispensed by the Hebrew sages and granted permission to drink the wine of a Gentile king, namely Artaxerxes, whose cupbearer he was as "Athersatha" — because Athersatha is not a Hebrew name but a Chaldean one, given by the Chaldeans.

FOR ALL THE PEOPLE WEPT WHEN THEY HEARD THE WORDS OF THE LAW — because they saw that the threats of the law, namely calamities of every kind, had befallen them on account of their transgression of it, and that they had continued for the most part in this state from the beginning of the Babylonian captivity up to the twentieth year of Artaxerxes, a span of many years.
GO, EAT RICH FOOD — Thus Christian law forbids fasting on Sunday, on account of the joy of the feast. So St. Thomas, Summa II-II, q. 147, art. 5, ad 3.

DO NOT BE GRIEVED — because although it is lawful to mourn and weep, yet on this feast day of the New Moon, on which we recall the benefits of God — both ancient and new and continually bestowed upon us — we ought to rejoice, not mourn.

THE JOY OF THE LORD IS YOUR STRENGTH. — The joy of the Lord can be understood in two ways. First, actively: it is the joy with which the Lord rejoices when He sees us bearing adversity with a strong and great spirit, not a small and abject one. For this thought greatly spurs pious souls, in whatever adversity and tribulation, not to let their spirit sink but to strengthen it, so that they may rejoice and exult in it, if they consider that this is pleasing to God, and that God rejoices and delights in our fortitude and joy in adversity — so Sanchez. Second, passively: the joy of the Lord is that which is conceived from the Lord, or from love, reverence, imitation, and obedience of the Lord; as if to say: God rejoices on feast days because on them He is more worshipped, adored, and invoked by the faithful; and therefore He likewise desires that the faithful imitate and put on this joy of His, and rejoice on the feast day, and sacrifice to Him as they rejoice, offering peace-offerings from which they may eat joyfully and feast, as it were, together with God Himself. For by this joy is acquired strength of mind and body — both for fulfilling the law of God, and for bearing bravely every adversity by which we are now oppressed; indeed, through this joy adversities are to be endured and dispersed, and removed through God. For if God sees us joyful and glad, and thereby strong and steadfast in devotion to His law and feast days, He will remove from us the plagues which, on account of the sad and pusillanimous neglect of them, He has sent upon us. For just as sadness makes men mournful, fainthearted, and sluggish, so joy on the contrary makes them cheerful, magnanimous, and eager worshippers of God and keepers of the law. Hence Vatablus translates: "The joy of the Lord is your strength," and explains it thus: "Strengthened by divine joy, you will be more courageous in spirit and more eager." For joy multiplies, arouses, sharpens, and diffuses the vital and animal spirits throughout the whole body, so as to invigorate, excite, and render it prompt for work; whereas sadness weakens, constricts, burdens, and withdraws those same spirits, so that the whole man is [left enfeebled].

CHAPTER NINE. SYNOPSIS OF THE CHAPTER. THE JEWS IN FASTING AND SACKCLOTH CONFESS THEIR SINS TO GOD. THE LEVITES CELEBRATE THE ANCIENT BENEFITS BY WHICH GOD CONTENDED WITH THE MISDEEDS OF THE PEOPLE: AND THEY RENEW THE COVENANT BETWEEN GOD AND THE PEOPLE.
On the twenty-fourth day of this month the children of Israel assembled in fasting and in sackcloth, and with earth upon them.
And the seed of the children of Israel separated themselves from all the children of strangers: and they stood and confessed their sins, and the iniquities of their fathers.
And they rose up to stand: and they read in the volume of the law of the Lord their God four times in the day, and four times they confessed and adored the Lord their God.
And there arose upon the step Josue, and Bani, and Cedmihel, Sabania, Bonni, Sarebia, Bani and Chanani: and they cried with a loud voice to the Lord their God.

And the Levites Josue and Cedmihel, Bonni, Hasebnia, Serebia, Odaia, Sebnia, Phaltahia said: Arise, bless the Lord your God from eternity to eternity: and let them bless the name of thy glory with all blessing and praise.

Thou thyself, O Lord, alone, thou hast made heaven, and the heaven of heavens, and all the host thereof: the earth and all things that are in it: the seas and all things that are in them: and thou givest life to all these things, and the host of heaven adoreth thee.
Thou thyself, O Lord God, didst choose Abram, and didst bring him forth from the fire of the Chaldeans, and didst give him the name of Abraham.

And thou foundest his heart faithful before thee: and thou madest a covenant with him to give him the land of the Chanaanite, the Hethite, and the Amorrhite, and the Pherezite, and the Jebusite, and the Gergesite, to give it to his seed: and thou hast fulfilled thy words, because thou art just.
And thou sawest the affliction of our fathers in Egypt, and thou heardest their cry by the Red Sea.

And thou gavest signs and wonders against Pharaoh, and against all his servants, and against all the people of his land: for thou knewest that they dealt proudly against them: and thou madest thyself a name, as it is at this day.
And thou didst divide the sea before them, and they passed through the midst of the sea on dry land: and their persecutors thou threwest into the deep, as a stone into mighty waters.
And in a pillar of a cloud thou wast their leader by day, and in a pillar of fire by night, that they might see the way by which they went.
Thou camest down also to mount Sinai, and didst speak with them from heaven, and thou gavest them right judgments, and the law of truth, ceremonies and good precepts.
And thou madest known to them thy holy sabbath, and didst prescribe to them commandments and ceremonies and the law, by the hand of Moses thy servant.

And thou gavest them bread from heaven in their hunger, and didst bring forth water from the rock for them in their thirst, and thou saidst to them that they should go in and possess the land, over which thou hadst lifted up thy hand to give them.
But they and our fathers dealt proudly, and hardened their necks, and hearkened not to thy commandments.

And they would not hear, and they remembered not thy wonders which thou hadst done for them. And they hardened their necks, and they gave their head to return to their bondage, as it were by contention. But thou, a forgiving God, gracious and merciful, longsuffering and full of compassion, didst not forsake them.
Yea, even when they had made to themselves a molten calf, and said: This is thy God that brought thee out of Egypt: and had committed great blasphemies:

Yet thou in thy manifold mercies didst not leave them in the desert: the pillar of the cloud departed not from them by day to lead them in the way, and the pillar of fire by night to shew them the way by which they should go.
And thou gavest them thy good Spirit to teach them, and thy manna thou withheldest not from their mouth, and thou gavest them water in their thirst.
Forty years didst thou feed them in the desert, and nothing was wanting to them: their garments grew not old, and their feet were not worn.
And thou gavest them kingdoms and peoples, and didst divide lots for them: and they possessed the land of Sehon, and the land of the king of Hesebon, and the land of Og king of Basan.
And thou didst multiply their children as the stars of heaven

Note here three offices of penitence, and likewise their symbols. The first is to sprinkle the head with ashes, as if confessing that we are vile and made of ashes, having been formed from dust and destined to return to dust, according to Gen. 3:19: "Dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return." The second is public and solemn fasting. The third is sackcloth or haircloth.

The seed of Israel was separated from all foreigners — that is, they separated themselves from gentile wives, and separated the sons they had begotten of these from the sons begotten of Israelite wives, in the manner described in Ezra 10:3.

And they (the Levites) arose to stand — in an elevated place, from which they could be seen and heard by the people. "And four times they made confession" — that is, at each reading they sang psalms and hymns to God, by which they celebrated God's beneficence, confessed the evil deeds of an ungrateful people, and as suppliants sought pardon for them from God.
The heaven of heavens — that is, the highest, vastest, and most excellent heaven, namely the Empyrean; and all their host, that is, all the angels and all the stars.

Thou didst choose Abraham and didst bring him out of the fire of the Chaldeans. One might object: the Hebrew and the Septuagint have "from Ur of the Chaldeans," and our translator renders it thus in Gen. 11:31 and elsewhere. I reply: Ur or Ura, as Josephus attests, is the proper name of a city of the Chaldeans, from which God led Abraham forth, which Eupolemus in Eusebius, Praep. lib. 9, calls Camirinem. It seems to have been named from the worship of fire (for this is what the Hebrew "ur" signifies), which the Chaldeans, as St. Jerome attests in his Questions on Genesis, worshipped as a god, just as Heliopolis in Egypt was named from the worship of the sun — for Heliopolis is the same as ἡλίου πόλις, that is, "city of the sun." Therefore our translator, in order to render the meaning of the Hebrew name "ur," translates it as "fire." Hence also certain Bibles write "Ignis" here with a capital I, as if it were a proper name of a city; for there is certainly here an allusion to Gen. 11:31, where it is said: "I brought them out of Ur of the Chaldeans." So too elsewhere our translator frequently renders the etymologies of proper Hebrew names, because in them lies hidden some mystery, or force and energy, as in Judg. 15:19, Gen. 26:20 and 22. Hence secondly, "ur" can be taken not as a proper name but as a common noun, signifying fire, that is, tribulation and affliction, as in Ps. 16:3 and Ps. 65:12. For Josephus, Antiq. 1.8, and St. Augustine, De Civ. Dei 16.13, attest that Abraham suffered many afflictions from the Chaldeans because he refused to worship fire, which they venerated as a god, as did the Persians. Indeed it is a Hebrew tradition that for this reason Abraham was cast into a fire but was miraculously delivered by God — a tradition approved by St. Jerome in his Questions on Genesis. Nor is it surprising that Josephus, Philo, and St. Paul (Heb. 11) make no mention of this (as Pererius objects), since they generally record only what is found in Scripture, as Josephus frequently professes of himself. See what was said on Gen. 11:31.

And thou gavest signs and wonders against Pharaoh — namely the ten plagues, with which God struck Pharaoh to compel him to release the Hebrews into Canaan. Here the Levites celebrate the magnificence and beneficence of God by recounting all that God did for the Hebrews in Egypt and Canaan: namely that he afflicted Egypt with ten plagues for their sake, that he gave them a pillar of fire as a guide on the way, likewise manna, the Law, water from the rock, and the land of Canaan. Wherefore they confess equally the ingratitude of the disobedient people. Thus this confession and prayer of theirs is as it were a compendium of the Pentateuch, Judges, and Kings.
And thou gavest them bread from heaven — namely manna, concerning which see Exodus 16.
Thou didst lift up thine hand — that is, thou didst swear. For we swear with the hand lifted up, as though calling God to witness.

And they gave a head — Vatablus: and they had chosen, that is, they wished to choose a leader who might bring them back into Egypt to their former servitude, Numbers 14. A second and simpler interpretation: they gave a head to turn back, that is, with hardened neck, as preceded, and with obstinate head they resolutely wished to return into Egypt. Thus at v. 29 it is said: They gave a withdrawing shoulder and hardened their neck.

Turn not away from thy face (but look continually with benign eyes, that thou mayest have compassion on us and show mercy) "all the labour" (all our afflictions) "in the days of the king of Assur" — who in the sixth year of Hezekiah carried the ten Tribes captive into Assyria.
Thou hast performed truth — that is, thou hast truly fulfilled, truly exhibited the rewards which thou hadst promised, and the punishments with which thou hadst threatened transgressors.
The harvests are multiplied for the kings — as if to say: Judaea indeed brings forth many harvests, but not for us, but for the kings of the Persians, to whom we are compelled to pay them as tribute.

Above all these things therefore — as if to say: On account of all these things therefore, that thou mayest have mercy on us, behold with a new promise and covenant we pledge ourselves that, having renounced idols and crimes, we will henceforth serve thee alone faithfully and constantly and perpetually.

And we write — the letters of this covenant which we enter into with thee, and we will cause it to be subscribed and sealed in the name of all by our Princes, whose names are recorded in the following chapter.

SYNOPSIS OF THE CHAPTER: There are enumerated those who signed the covenant of the people made with God, who promise to keep all the commandments of God, and specifically the Sabbath and the seventh year; likewise to pay the tithes and firstfruits appointed by the Law, in which matters they had hitherto been negligent and transgressors.
The signatories were Nehemiah the Athersatha son of Hachelai, and Zedekiah.
Seraiah, Azariah, Jeremiah.
Pashur, Amariah, Malchijah.
Hattush, Shebaniah, Malluch.
Harim, Meremoth, Obadiah.
Daniel, Ginnethon, Baruch.
Meshullam, Abijah, Mijamin.
Maaziah, Bilgai, Shemaiah: these were the priests.
Now the Levites: Jeshua son of Azaniah, Binnui of the sons of Henadad, Kadmiel.
And their brethren: Shebaniah, Hodaiah, Kelita, Hanan.
Micha, Rehob, Hashabiah.
Zaccur, Sherebiah, Shebaniah.
Hodaiah, Bani, Beninu.
The heads of the people: Parosh, Pahath-moab, Elam, Zatthu, Bani.
Bunni, Azgad, Bebai.
Adonijah, Bigvai, Adin.
Ater, Hezekiah, Azzur.
Hodaiah, Hashum, Bezai.
Hariph, Anathoth, Nebai.
Magpiash, Meshullam, Hezir.
Meshizabel, Zadok, Jaddua.
Pelatiah, Hanan, Anaiah.
Hoshea, Hananiah, Hasshub.
[The signatories of the covenant of the people with God were: Nehemiah (as leader), Athersatha, son of Hachelai.] This Nehemiah is distinct from the one who was also called Athersatha.
[And all who separated themselves from the peoples of the lands unto the law of God,] that is, who were converted from paganism to Judaism, and became Proselytes.
[All who could understand.] Refer this to the sons: as if to say, All the sons of proselytes who were not infants but adults and possessed of reason also signed this covenant.

[Sponding (they were) for their brothers, the chief men of them,] as if to say, in so great a number not all could sign this covenant; wherefore the chief men alone swore and signed on behalf of their fellow tribesmen.
[And who (like the chief men, were notable among the people) came to promise and to swear.] The Hebrew adds: with a curse, saying: May God punish us if we do not keep His commandments.

[We will remit the seventh year,] that is, the sabbatical year; as if to say, We will allow the land to keep sabbath in the seventh year, so that we do not cultivate the fields, but leave what grows spontaneously in them to the poor, according to the law of Exodus 23:11.

[And the exaction of every hand,] as if to say, We will likewise remit to debtors those things which are owed to us by the bond of their hands, namely so that in the seventh year we do not exact them. For the law equally commanded this, that, since the land was not cultivated in the seventh year, debts payable from the fruits of the land should likewise not be paid in that same year.
[And in the sanctified things,] that is, in the peace offerings which are offered to God; for he distinguishes these from the victims for sin, of which he speaks immediately after.
[From the appointed times of the year even to the year,] that is, yearly, or in each individual year.
[The tenth part of the land,] that is, the tithes of the fruits of the land. For the Levites could not possess land, but only receive from it the tithes of its fruits.

[Of our works.] Hence it is clear that the Jews paid tithes not only of fields, but also of their labors and works, so that from the profit accruing to them therefrom they would give a tenth part to the Levites.

[And the priest, the son of Aaron, shall be with the Levites in the tithes of the Levites,] that is, the priests who are sons of Aaron shall have a share of the tithes along with the Levites, namely the tenth of the tenth. For as follows: the Levites shall offer the tenth part of their tithe in the house of God. Therefore the Levites gave the tenth part of their tithes to the priests; the priests in turn gave the tenth of their tenth to the High Priest, who was therefore very rich and wealthy, as I said at Numbers 18.

[There shall be the sanctified vessels, and the priests, and the singers, and the doorkeepers, and the ministers,] while they minister in the temple during the time of their course. For they dwelt in the chambers annexed to the temple, which were called exedrae and gazophylacia, and were sustained there; for by the expense of the colonists and Levites, the firstfruits which fell to the priests were to be brought to these treasuries. The Hebrews therefore pledge that they will diligently attend to all these things, which they had previously neglected.

[And we will not forsake the house of God,] as if to say, We will take care of all things which pertain to the uses of the temple and to the sustenance of the priests and Levites. These things therefore they swear they will do, because in them they had hitherto been negligent.

Tobiah the Ammonite, though not of the gods of the nations but rich and powerful, had contracted affinity with Eliashib the High Priest through marriage; for which reason Eliashib had set him over the treasury of the temple as his kinsman — which was a great dignity and advantage for Tobiah, but a greater indignity and harm for Israel, since an Ammonite servant was being raised to such dignity in Israel contrary to the law cited at Deut. 23:3 in v.1.

There was also harm because Tobiah diverted the treasury not to the uses of the priests and the temple, for which it was instituted, but to his own advantage, storing all his household goods therein. Nehemiah, judging this most unworthy, expelled him from the treasury and restored it to Israel.

He made for himself a great treasury-chamber, and before it they were storing gifts, and frankincense, and vessels, etc. — that is, Eliashib set Tobiah the Ammonite over all the treasury of the temple, namely the first-fruits and tithes and other things brought to the Levites and Priests.
That he might make for him a treasury — namely the treasury-chamber already mentioned, as the Septuagint has it, in which the treasures of the temple were kept. This is clear from the Hebrew.
I cast out the vessels — that is, the household goods of Tobiah.
And I restored there the vessels of the house of God and the sacrifice — that is, the things pertaining to sacrifice, namely oil, wine, fine flour, salt, etc.
I made them stand in their stations — that is, I restored the Levites and singers to their proper places and duties.

When the gates of Jerusalem were at rest on the Sabbath day — that is, from their duty of sending out and admitting those who entered. Hebrew: "when the gates were shadowed," that is, when the evening of the sixth day arrived; for then the Sabbath began, as I have already said. Hence the Septuagint renders: "before the day," namely the artificial day, of the Sabbath. Nehemiah therefore ordered the gates to be shut for the entire natural day of the Sabbath, through 24 hours, lest anyone by carrying burdens through them should violate the Sabbath.

That no one might bring in a burden — through the gates. Hence a gate is called "porta" because burdens are imported and exported through it, as Cato says, and from him Isidore, Etymologiae 15.2; though Donatus would have "porta" derive from the carrying of the plough, because, he says, in the laying out of cities, when the one designating the city drove yoked cow and bull, he would lift the plough by hand at the gate, so as not to make a furrow where the city's entrances were to be left.
I will lay my hand upon you — that is, I will seize and punish you, and the burdens of goods which you bring in I will confiscate.
